title: Cropped 15 July 2026: Uganda starves | Trump opens endangered habitats | UK cuts rainforest aid - Carbon Brief
author: Carbon Brief Staff
gist: Fortnightly land/food/nature roundup: drought-driven starvation in Uganda and an FAO/WFP $200m El Niño appeal, the Trump administration's rollback of Endangered Species Act habitat protections to open federal land to drilling/mining, JBS scrapping its Scope 3 net-zero commitment, and a detailed spotlight on the UK slashing its Congo basin rainforest funding (CBFA budget cut 79% to £18.8m).

themes.theme: El Niño-driven food and drought crisis

insights.claim: A prolonged drought following a recent heatwave has caused at least 16 hunger-related deaths and significant crop losses in Uganda, with forecasters warning a developing El Niño could later bring flooding to the same drought-hit areas.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Signals acute humanitarian and commodity-supply risk in East Africa; investors in agricultural commodities and African sovereign/aid-linked debt should track El Niño's whipsaw drought-to-flood pattern.

insights.claim: The UN Food and Agriculture Organization and World Food Programme have appealed for $200m to help 22 high-risk African nations cope with El Niño impacts via cash transfers, climate-resilient seeds, livestock protection and flood control, while analysts warn El Niño could cause a severe global food-price shock lasting into 2028.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A multi-year global food-price shock forecast (into 2028) is directly relevant to agricultural commodity, fertilizer and food-inflation-linked positioning.

insights.claim: India recorded its driest June in 12 years and France saw a 'double-digit production' decline in agriculture, while UK farmers are adopting no-till, chemical-free practices to improve soil water retention in response to extreme heat.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Confirms extreme-weather-driven yield stress across multiple major agricultural producers simultaneously in mid-2026, a bullish signal for grain and food-commodity price forecasts.

themes.theme: Trump rollback of endangered species protections

insights.claim: The Trump administration reversed decades of Endangered Species Act enforcement by redefining what constitutes 'harm' to species, removing the prohibition on habitat modification or degradation and opening sensitive federal habitats to drilling, mining, farming and real estate development.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Materially expands the addressable federal land base for US oil, gas and mining permitting -- a regulatory tailwind for extractive-sector project pipelines, though under active legal challenge.

insights.claim: US environmental groups have sued the Trump government over the Endangered Species Act change, and legal scholars say the administration acted without conducting scientific research into its impact, while the National Mining Association applauded the announcement.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Active litigation creates near-term legal uncertainty for any project relying on the new rule; mining sector explicitly welcomes the change as a permitting tailwind.
insights.quote: common sense, biological science and federal law

insights.claim: JBS, the world's largest meat supplier, scrapped a key Scope 3 climate goal covering supplier emissions -- which make up the vast bulk of its environmental footprint -- citing difficulty controlling these indirect emissions.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A high-profile Scope 3 target abandonment by the largest global meat company signals weakening corporate climate commitments in the agriculture/protein sector, relevant to ESG screening and supply-chain emissions risk assessments.

insights.claim: Pacific gray whales face a 'catastrophic die-off' as sea-ice loss disrupts their food sources, and conservationists warn more than half of deep-sea vent-clustering molluscs could face extinction from deep-sea mining.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Adds to the growing body of biodiversity-loss evidence that could inform regulatory scrutiny of deep-sea mining permits, a nascent but capital-intensive extractive sector.

insights.claim: Deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon fell to its lowest level in a decade under President Lula, but his plan to end illegal deforestation by 2030 could be jeopardized if he is not re-elected.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Brazilian election outcomes are a direct swing factor for Amazon deforestation trajectory and carbon-market/forest-carbon-credit supply from the region.

themes.theme: UK Congo basin rainforest funding collapse

insights.claim: The UK has slashed the Congo basin forest action programme's budget by 79%, from £90m to £18.8m, as part of Labour's aid cuts -- this programme was explicitly designed to provide roughly half of the UK's £200m COP26 pledge to the Congo basin, the world's second-largest rainforest.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of donor-country climate-finance retrenchment for rainforest conservation, raising risk for forest-carbon and biodiversity-credit projects dependent on bilateral aid in the Congo basin.

insights.claim: As of 2024, the UK had provided only £39.8m of its £200m COP26 Congo basin pledge, and a separate £12.3m Congo biodiverse landscapes fund project was closed five years ahead of schedule.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Quantifies the funding shortfall (roughly 20% delivered) against a headline pledge, useful for assessing credibility risk in future donor climate-finance commitments.

insights.claim: Former Conservative COP26 minister Zac Goldsmith says the UK has gone from being a leader on international nature protection to 'breaking promises' to countries that made long-term development decisions in expectation that partners would honour their commitments.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A former-government insider's public rebuke of UK climate-finance credibility signals reputational and diplomatic risk that could affect UK standing in future COP negotiations and bilateral trade/aid relationships.
insights.quote: We have gone from being perhaps the leader on protecting nature internationally to breaking promises to countries around the world.

themes.theme: Energy security fallout from Iran conflict

insights.claim: The International Energy Agency warned that disruption in the Strait of Hormuz from the Iran war has stunted supplies and raised prices of liquefied petroleum gas, threatening Africa's clean cooking transition efforts.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Links Middle East conflict escalation directly to African energy-poverty and clean-cooking policy outcomes, a second-order geopolitical risk for LPG-dependent markets and clean-cooking investment programs.
